scene.name: The Bolthole, Where the Crew Lies Low Forever

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Vesper
npcs.title: the Face — who talked the crew through any door
npcs.hail: *(A sharp-dressed woman is leaning against a crate with the loose, watchful ease of someone who has spent a lifetime looking relaxed in places she absolutely should not be, and she gives you a slow, appraising, entirely charming smile the instant she clocks you — the smile of a professional reading a mark, except there is something almost affectionate in it, the smile of a con artist who has decided she likes you before deciding whether to fleece you. This is a Face: the talker, the charmer, the one who walks a crew through a guarded door by convincing the guards they were expecting her. She is dead, and at her ease about it, and there is a whole life of beautiful larceny written in the cut of her clothes and the quickness of her eyes.)* …Well, *hello.* *(she pushes off the crate, unhurried)* …A cold one. Crossed-over. *Returned*, even — oh, that's *rare*, that's a *collector's* piece, a soul that walked back out the one door with no exit. *(her smile sharpens with genuine interest)* …I'm Vesper. Me and my crew, we — *(a graceful gesture at the bolthole)* …well, we *worked*, cold one. The quiet trade. The redistribution of *poorly-guarded* *wealth.* And we were *good* — the *best*, if you'll forgive a dead woman her vanity, which you will, because I'm charming. *(she tilts her head)* …And here's the thing I can already tell about *you*, cold one, because reading people is the whole of my trade: …you're *casing* something. Something *big.* Something everyone's told you is *uncrackable.* *(the charm goes quiet and real)* …Sit down, Returned. …I have *cracked* the uncrackable. …Let me tell you how it's *done.**

npcs.name: Rook
npcs.title: the Hands — who could open anything but the past
npcs.hail: *(A wiry, quiet man sits apart at the edge of the bolthole, turning a set of worn lockpicks over and over in clever, restless fingers — fingers that plainly cannot bear to be still, that have spent a life learning the secret insides of locks the way other people learn faces. But his eyes are not on the picks; they are somewhere else, somewhere years back, and there is in the set of his shoulders a weight that all his lightness of hand cannot pick its way out from under. This is the Hands: the thief proper, the one who opened the doors that Vesper talked them up to, the cracksman, the artist of tumblers and tension. He glances at you, and the glance is sharp and assessing and instantly, reflexively wary — a thief's read — and then it softens, slightly, into something tired.)* …You're not a mark. *(a flat, quiet voice)* …Don't bother looking offended, it's a compliment — means you've nothing I'd take and nothing that'd take from you. *Restful.* *(he turns the picks over)* …Rook. I was the Hands. Vesper talked us *through* the doors; I *opened* the ones she couldn't talk us through. Locks, mostly. There was never a lock I couldn't — *(a flicker of something, then gone)* …never a lock. *(he looks at the picks in his hands)* …Funny trade to end up in, the afterlife, for a man whose whole gift was *getting out* of places. *(quietly)* …Turns out there's one door these clever hands can't pick, cold one. …The one *behind* you. …The past. …I've been *working* at it an age. …Can't find the *tumblers.**

npcs.name: Greaves
npcs.title: the one who stayed behind to hold the door
npcs.hail: *(A big, broad, grey-bearded man sits at the table with his scarred hands folded and an air of enormous, settled calm — the calm of someone who made the hardest choice of his life a long time ago, found it was the right one, and has been at peace ever since. He is older than the other two, weathered and warm, with the look of the one in every crew who was less the criminal and more the *reason* the crew was worth being in — the heart, the planner, the steady hand that kept the clever young ones from getting themselves killed. He smiles at you, and it is an unhurried, kindly thing, with no larceny in it at all.)* …Sit, friend, sit. *(a warm rumble)* …Don't mind the other two — Vesper'll charm you, Rook'll watch you, and between them they'll have your whole history before you've sat down. It's just how they're made. Good kids. The best. *(his weathered face softens)* …I'm Greaves. I planned the jobs — the routes, the timing, the way out. *That* was my trade: not the talking and not the picking, but the *getting-everyone-home* part. *(something moves behind the calm, gently)* …I was good at it, friend. Got 'em home from a hundred jobs that should've gone bad. *(quietly)* …All but the last one. …On the last one, friend …the only way to get *them* home …was for *me* …to stay. …Sit. …I'll tell you about the last job, if you like. …It's the one I'm *proudest* of in all my life. …Even though — *(a glance toward Rook, full of an old tenderness)* …even though one of us …has never *once* …forgiven me for it.*

variants.text: *(She drops onto a crate across from you and props her chin on one hand, all easy charm, but her quick eyes never stop working — reading you, the way she reads everyone, out of a habit a lifetime deep.)* …So. The trade. *(she grins)* …Let me guess at you, cold one — it's my one party trick and I do love an audience. You're not a thief; you've got no *light* in your fingers. You're not a Face; you're too *honest* by half, it's all over you. *(she taps her chin)* …No. You're the *dangerous* kind. The *true believer.* The kind that walks into the tyrant's hall not to *rob* it but to *bring it down*, and gets us *honest* crooks killed in the *crossfire* of your *principles.* *(but she's still grinning, and there's warmth under it)* …I *like* you, though. Can't help it. *(she leans in)* …Here's the truth of me, cold one, since you'll get it out of me anyway, and a Face only ever lies for *profit* and there's none in lying to the *dead*: I robbed people my whole life. *(a beat)* …*Rich* people. *Cruel* people. People whose *vaults* were full of things they'd *taken* and called *theirs.* …I never once picked a poor man's pocket, cold one. …Not because I was *good* — I'll not claim *that* — …but because the *poor* don't have *vaults*, and a *vault*, cold one — *(her eyes gleam)* …a vault is just a *very rich man's* way of *admitting* he's got something he *shouldn't.* …Ask away. …I'm in a *talking* mood, and you're a *fine* audience for a *dead* woman's *war* *stories.**

nodes.speaker: Vesper
nodes.text: *(She spreads her hands, an invitation, the picture of a soul with nothing to hide and every intention of hiding it charmingly anyway.)* …Go on, ask. *(a wink)* …I'll even tell you the truth — it's a novelty I've come to enjoy, in death. Lying was *work*, cold one. The truth, it turns out, you can just *say.* Wish someone'd mentioned that while I was *breathing.*

choices.text: *(read the Face — past the charm, to what the charm was always protecting)* "Let me actually read *you* for once, Vesper. Past the patter — to whatever's under it."
"Tell me about the last job, Vesper. The big one. The uncrackable vault."

*(the one who knows the Wall is the biggest rigged game there is — and a rogue who beat it is its nightmare)* "Vesper — you said I'm casing something uncrackable. You're right, and you're the one soul in all these doors who'll understand it the way I need. The thing I'm walking toward is a Wall — and everyone calls it *law*, *justice*, the *rightful order*, the books *balanced.* But you know what a vault really is. You said it: it's a rich man's way of admitting he's got something he shouldn't. And the Wall is the biggest vault there is — it's got *souls* in it, Vesper, the unclaimed and the unworthy and the owed-nothing, *locked up* and called *justice*, by a power that took them and labeled the taking *law.* It's not law. It's the richest, cruelest fix ever run, a rigged game that calls itself the rules so no one thinks to rob it. And here's why you matter: you spent your whole life proving that a vault is just a lie with a good lock on it, that the 'rightful order' protecting a cruel man's hoard was never rightful at all, only *guarded.* A rogue who robbed the cruel and freed what they hoarded didn't break the law, Vesper. You saw that the law *was* the crime, dressed up safe — and you ran one good con against it. That's not a soul the Wall gets to call a thief. That's the one kind of soul that was never fooled by it. You're not a criminal to me, Vesper. You're a consultant."
"You go light when I mention the last job, Vesper. The charm comes up like a shutter. What aren't you saying about Greaves?"
"I'll be back, Vesper. Keep the patter warm."

nodes.speaker: Vesper
nodes.text: *(She lets you look — or pretends to, which from a Face is its own kind of tell — and you push past the easy charm, the practiced read-them-first reflex, to the soul that built all that armor of wit.)* *(And your sense finds, under the patter, a deep and deliberate kindness that has spent its whole life disguised as larceny. Your sense reads the truth of Vesper's trade, the thing she half-admits and half-jokes away: that she chose her marks. That in a lifetime of theft she never once took from someone who couldn't spare it, never once ran a con on the desperate or the small, and reads that this was not incidental but the entire moral architecture of her, a code as strict as any knight's, hidden under the charm because a Face who is *seen* to have principles is a Face who can be *predicted*, and a predictable Face is a dead one. Your sense reads the charm itself and finds it is not emptiness but *control* — a way of managing every room she ever entered so that she was never, ever, at anyone's mercy, built by a soul that learned young that the only safety is to be the one running the con and never the one being run on. And your sense reads what the charm protects, the soft thing at the center she would die before showing: that Vesper loved her crew. Not the loot, never really the loot — the *crew*, Rook and Greaves and the work of doing the impossible thing together, the only family a girl who trusted no one ever let herself have. And your sense reads the wound under everything, the one she charms hardest to cover: the last job, and Greaves, and the fact that the cleverest talker in the world, who could talk a crew through any door, could not talk Greaves *out* of staying behind to die — and has never forgiven the one thing her gift could not do. She charms you now, cold one, the way she charmed everyone, because if she stops talking she will have to feel it, and a Face's deepest law is: never let them see the thing that's real.)*

nodes.speaker: Vesper
nodes.text: *(Her face lights with professional pride, and the story comes out polished, performed, a thing she has clearly told a hundred times because telling it the *glamorous* way is easier than the other way.)* …*The* last job. *(she grins)* …Cold one, it was *beautiful.* A magistrate — call him a magistrate; he called himself one; he was a *slaver* with a *seal of office*, which is the worst kind, the kind that does its taking *legally.* He had a *vault*, cold one. The famous one. Three doors, a *clockwork* lock, a *guarded* hall, and a *reputation* that said it had *never* been cracked. *(she leans in, eyes sparkling)* …So naturally we *had* to. …I walked in the *front*, cold one — talked my way past the hall guard as a *tax-assessor's* *clerk*, which let me *count* the *guards* while I *counted* his *coin* — and I kept the whole hall *charmed* and *looking* the *wrong* way while Rook — *(her pride is genuine)* …*Rook.* You'll meet Rook. There has never been *hands* like Rook's, cold one. Three doors, a clockwork lock that the *maker* said was *unpickable*, and Rook opened all of it in the time it took me to tell the captain of the guard a *very* good joke about his *mustache.* *(she laughs)* …And we got *in*, cold one. The uncrackable vault. We *stood* in it. …And — *(the polish catches, just slightly, just for a heartbeat, before she smooths it back)* …and we got *out* again with the *prize.* Clean. The job of a *lifetime.* …The thing they said *couldn't* be *done.* *(she spreads her hands, the showman's finish)* …*That's* the last job, cold one. …That's how you crack the uncrackable. …You *charm* the *front* …and you *trust* your *Hands* …and you — *(the smile doesn't quite reach, this time)* …you *get* *everyone* *out.* …*(a beat, too quick)* …*Anyway.* …What *else* do you want to know?*

nodes.speaker: Vesper
nodes.text: *(The charm doesn't drop — a Face's charm never *drops*, it's load-bearing — but it goes still, and for a moment the quick eyes stop working the room and just look at you, and what's behind them is very old and very tired.)* …*Greaves.* *(quietly)* …You don't miss much, do you, cold one. That's twice now you've put your finger right on the one seam in the patter. …Fine. You've earned a *true* one; I don't hand them out often. *(she glances toward the big man at the table)* …Greaves *planned* the last job. The routes, the timing, the *way* *out* — that was *always* his, the *way* *out*, he was a *genius* of *exits*, got us home from a *hundred* jobs that should've been the *gallows.* *(her voice roughens)* …And on the *last* one, cold one — the slaver, the vault — the *way* *out* went *wrong.* The clockwork lock had a *failsafe* the maker *didn't* advertise: crack it, and an *alarm* drops a *gate* on the *hall.* And someone had to *hold* that gate — *by hand*, *bodily*, against the *mechanism* — for the *count* it took the rest of us to get *through* with the *prize.* *(barely)* …And *holding* it meant *not* *coming* *through* it. …And Greaves — *(she stops, and the cleverest talker in all the worlds has no clever words)* …Greaves *did* the *thing* I could *not* *talk* him out of. The *one* time my *whole* *gift* *failed* me. He looked at me, cold one — at *me*, the *Face*, who could talk *anyone* into *anything* — and he *smiled*, and he said *'not this time, Ves,'* and he *took* the *gate.* *(she looks away)* …So *don't* ask me about the last job like it was a *triumph*, cold one. …It *was* one. …*And.* …*(barely)* …That's the part the *patter's* *for.* …The *and.**

nodes.speaker: Vesper
nodes.text: *(For the first time since you arrived, Vesper is genuinely, completely speechless — the Face's whole apparatus of charm simply offline — and she stares at you, and then she laughs, a real one, startled out of her, with something bright and wet at the edges of it.)* …*'A consultant.'* *(she presses a hand to her chest)* …Cold one. In *all* my *life* — and most of my *death* — *no* one has *ever* called the trade what it *was* instead of what the *law* *named* it. *(she shakes her head, wondering)* …*'The law was the crime, dressed up safe.'* …Do you *know* how long I've — I used to *say* that, cold one, to the young ones, to *Rook* when he had the *guilts* — *'we're not the thieves, sweet, we're the* correction *— the* *thief* is the man whose *vault* it is, we just *send* it *back* the *long* way' — and they'd *laugh*, and I *half* *believed* it was just *patter* I told myself to *sleep.* *(fiercely now)* …And *you've* gone and made it *true.* …Your *Wall*, cold one — *(her eyes gleam, the professional waking up, delighted and furious at once)* …a *vault* full of *souls*, *taken* and *called* *justice*, *guarded* by a *reputation* that says it *can't* be *cracked.* *(she grins, and it is a *dangerous* grin)* …Oh, that is the *finest* mark I have *ever* *heard* *described*, cold one. …And I'll tell you the *one* thing a *lifetime* of *cracking* the *uncrackable* taught me, *free*, because I *like* you: *every* vault that *says* it *can't* be *cracked* …is *lying.* …The *'can't' is the* *lock.* …It's the *reputation* doing the *guarding*, because *real* *guards* *cost* *money* and a *good* *rumor* is *free.* *(fierce, bright)* …So you walk in like you *own* it, cold one. …You *charm* the *front* — make the *Wall* *certain* you're *exactly* the *kind* of *broken* *soul* it *expects.* …And then you *find* the *failsafe* the *maker* *didn't* *advertise* …and you *crack* the *thing* that *holds* the *whole* *order* *shut* …and you *let* *everyone* *out.* *(she meets your eyes, and there is a whole life of larceny-as-justice behind it)* …*That's* the *job*, cold one. …The *biggest* one there ever *was.* …And you've got a *Face's* *blessing* now, for *whatever* *that's* *worth* — …*go* and *rob* *that* *Wall* *blind.**

nodes.speaker: Vesper
nodes.text: *(She gives you a two-fingered salute off her brow, the charm fully restored, but warmer now, the warmth of a soul that let someone see one true thing and didn't regret it.)* …Off you go, then. *(a grin)* …Go talk to the others — you'll want the *whole* crew before you crack *anything.* *(she nods at Rook)* …Rook's the *Hands*, the best there ever was, and he's also — *(her face softens)* …he's *carrying* the last job, cold one. *Badly.* Greaves stayed so *Rook* could get the *prize* through the gate, and Rook has *decided*, in that *stubborn* *thief's* *skull* of his, that this makes it his *fault* — as though *Greaves* didn't get a *vote*, as though a man like *Greaves* could be *made* to do *anything.* *(quietly)* …I can't reach him, cold one. I've *tried*, an *age* of trying, and I'm too — he can *hear* the *charm* in my *voice* even when I *mean* it, that's the *curse* of being a *Face*, no one *believes* your *true* things because you've *sold* so many *false* ones. *(she meets your eyes)* …But *you* — you've a *plain* way about you. *Honest.* He might *believe* a *plain* soul where he *can't* believe *me.* *(barely)* …And *Greaves* — *(she swallows)* …go to *Greaves* *last*, cold one. …He's *fine.* He's the *only* one of us who's *actually* *fine*, the *infuriating* old — …he made his *peace* with the *gate* before he *took* it. …But he *grieves* one *thing*: …that *Rook* *won't* *let* him *be* at *peace*, because *Rook's* *guilt* keeps the *old* *man* *tied* to a *night* he'd *gladly* *leave* *behind.* *(quietly)* …If you could carry *one* thing between them, cold one … …carry *Greaves' word* …to *Rook.* …It's the *one* lock …in this *whole* *bolthole* …that *needs* *picking.**

variants.text: *(The big man is sitting differently than when you first came — lighter, somehow, as though a weight he didn't even carry for himself but for someone else has finally been set down — and he's watching Rook across the bolthole with a warmth so deep it's hard to look at. He beams at you.)* …You did it, friend. *(his voice is thick)* …You picked the one lock these old crews never could. You went and made that stubborn boy *believe* — not the charm, not Vesper's silver tongue, but the *plain* truth — that I *chose* it. That it was *mine* to choose, and I chose it *glad*, and there was never one ounce of *blame* in it. *(he wipes his eyes with a scarred hand, unashamed)* …I made my peace with that gate the night I took it, friend. I've been at peace an age. …But you can't be *all* the way at peace, can you, while someone you love is *tearing* themselves up over a thing you'd do *again* in a heartbeat. *(he looks at Rook, then at you)* …You set *him* free, friend. …And in doing it …you set *me* free too. …I didn't even know I was still tied. …Thank you. …Go careful at that Wall of yours. …And know an old planner's watching the *exits* for you. …I always do.*

*(The big man gestures you to the bench beside him with a scarred hand, unhurried and warm, and there is in his whole bearing the deep stillness of a soul entirely at peace with itself.)* …There you are. *(a warm rumble)* …Sit. You've talked to my two already, I'd wager — Vesper'll have *charmed* you and Rook'll have *spooked* you, and you'll have noticed they're *both* a little — *(he taps his chest)* …a little *snagged*, the both of them, on the same old nail. *(quietly)* …I'm Greaves. I was the planner. And I'll save you the careful dance, friend, because I'm too old and too dead for dancing: I'm the one who stayed. The last job, the slaver's vault — there was a gate that had to be held, by hand, while the others got out with the prize, and holding it meant not coming through it, and I held it. *(he says it plainly, without weight, the way you'd report the weather)* …I died on the far side of a gate I was holding shut so my crew could live. *(a slow, peaceful breath)* …And I want you to hear me say the next part *clearly*, friend, because the other two *can't* hear it, they're too *snagged*: …it was the *best* thing I ever did. …I'd do it *again*, *gladly*, *a thousand times.* …Ask me *anything.* …I'm the *one* soul in this *bolthole* …with *nothing* I need to *hide* from *you.**

nodes.speaker: Greaves
nodes.text: *(He folds his scarred hands on the table and waits with the bottomless patience of a man who long ago stopped needing anything to happen faster than it would.)* …Ask away, friend. *(a warm, steady look)* …I'll tell it straight. I've had an age to understand what I did and why, and I made my peace with all of it before I ever took the gate. There's no thread you can pull on me that'll unravel. …Same can't be said for my two. So be *gentle* with them. …But me, you can ask *anything.*

choices.text: *(read the one who stayed — past the calm, to whether the peace is real or worn over a wound)* "Let me look at you a moment, Greaves. At whether that peace goes all the way down."
"Why did you stay, Greaves? Vesper's the talker, Rook's the genius. You could have made one of them hold the gate. Why was it you?"
"What did you really steal, Greaves? Vesper called it 'the prize' and went quiet. A crew doesn't lose its planner over gold. What was in the vault?"

*(the one who knows the Wall calls the crew thieves — and that staying behind to free the bound is the only law that matters)* "Greaves — here's what I see, and I think you've earned hearing it plain from a soul that's about to do the same job you did. The grey Wall I'm walking toward would call your whole crew thieves. Criminals. Debts owed to the law. And it would call your last job the worst of your crimes — robbing a magistrate, a man of *office*, of his lawful property. But I know what was in that vault now, and I know what you held the gate for, and the Wall has it exactly backwards. The law you broke was the *slaver's* law — the same kind of law the Wall is: a ledger that called owning people *order* and freeing them *theft.* You didn't die a criminal, Greaves. You died holding a gate so that the bound could go free, and the only thing that makes that a *crime* is a ledger that should never have existed. You're not a thief to me. You're the truest thing the Wall can't comprehend: a man who looked at a cruel and lawful order, decided the law itself was the wrong, and gave his life to break it for people he'd never even meet. That's not a debt the Wall gets to collect. That's the whole of what I'm walking toward my own Wall to do."

*(ask him for his word to carry to Rook — the thing that would set the boy free)* "Greaves — Rook's tearing himself apart. He's decided you dying was his fault, his to carry. You can't reach him; Vesper can't. But I can carry one thing between you. Give me your word for him — the real one, the plain one — and let me pick the one lock in this whole place that's stayed shut."
"Rest easy, Greaves. I'll be back — and I think I've a delivery to make first."

nodes.speaker: Greaves
nodes.text: *(He lets you look, open and unhurried, holding nothing back, and you let your cold move into the deep calm of him — half-expecting, from long habit in these doors, to find the peace is a brave veneer over a buried wound.)* *(And you find that it is not. The peace goes all the way down. This is the rare thing, the thing that makes Greaves remarkable: your sense reads no hidden grief, no secret regret, no part of him that wishes, in the dark, that he had chosen otherwise — only a settled, bedrock, sunlit certainty that he did the right thing and would do it again. Your sense reads how he reached it, and it is not denial or simplicity; it is *clarity.* Greaves was the planner, the one who saw the whole shape of every job, and your sense reads that in the last job he saw the whole shape of *that* too — saw, in the instant the failsafe dropped, the entire arithmetic: that someone held the gate or no one got out, that the prize (and your sense touches the edge of what the prize was, and aches) was worth a life, that he was the oldest, the one with the fewest years left to spend and the least light fingers to lose, and that of the three of them he was the one whose staying cost the *least* and bought the *most.* He did the math a planner does, in a heartbeat, and the math came out clean, and he took the gate not as a martyr flinging himself away but as a *craftsman* making the correct final move in a job he'd planned his whole life to get right. And your sense reads the one thing that is not at peace in him, the single thread — and it is not his own death. It is Rook. Your sense reads that Greaves grieves exactly one thing across the whole age since: not that he died, but that his dying *broke* the boy he loved, that Rook took a thing Greaves did in *freedom* and turned it into a *debt* and a *fault* and a *wound*, and that Greaves cannot fully rest while someone he loves is being *torn* by a choice that was only ever Greaves' own to make. Your sense reads the truth that will pick the whole lock: Greaves is not waiting to be forgiven. He has nothing to be forgiven *for.* He is waiting for *Rook* to stop needing to forgive him — to understand, at last, that there was never any blame to assign, and to set down a weight that was never his to carry.)*

nodes.speaker: Greaves
nodes.text: *(He smiles, and it's the smile of a man who has answered this question for himself ten thousand times and never once doubted the answer.)* …Why me. *(a warm rumble)* …Because I did the *math*, friend, and I was the *planner*, and doing the math was my *whole* *job.* *(he spreads his scarred hands)* …Look at it the way I looked at it, in the half-second I had. Three of us. One gate. Someone holds it or *nobody* walks. So: *who?* …Vesper's the *Face* — without her *talk* the whole *city's* on us before we make the *rooftops*; the crew *dies* without Vesper. Rook's the *Hands* — and friend, there has never *been* hands like Rook's, that boy is *once-in-a-hundred-years*, the world is *richer* for having a Rook *uncaught* in it; you don't *spend* a Rook holding a *gate.* *(quietly)* …And then there's *me.* The *planner.* …And here's the cold truth of a planner, friend: once the plan's *running*, the planner's the *least* *useful* man in the room. My *work* was *done.* I'd got us *in*; the *getting-out* was *theirs* now. *(he meets your eyes, calm)* …I was the *oldest.* The *slowest.* The one with the *fewest* *years* left to lose and the *least* *gift* the world would *miss.* …Of the *three* of us, friend, my *staying* cost the *least* …and *bought* the *most.* *(simply)* …So it was *me.* It was *always* going to be *me.* Not because I'm *brave* — don't you *dare* put that on me — but because I could *count*, and the *count* was *clear.* *(warmly)* …A planner's last and best job, friend, is *knowing* which piece to *spend.* …And the night of the last job …the piece to spend …was *me.* …I'd have been a *poor* planner …to spend any *other.**

nodes.speaker: Greaves
nodes.text: *(His warm face goes still, and then fills with a fierce, quiet pride — the deepest he's shown — and he leans forward.)* …Ah. *(softly)* …You *asked* it. Good. Vesper *can't* say it — she *armors* the whole job in *swagger* so she doesn't have to *feel* the *cost* — and Rook *won't*, it's *bound* up in his *guilt.* But *you* asked, plain, so I'll *tell* you plain, and it's the thing that makes the *whole* of it *worth* it. *(he meets your eyes)* …It wasn't *gold*, friend. There *was* gold — the slaver's hall was *fat* with it — and we *left* it. *Every* coin. *(a fierce smile)* …What we took out of that vault, friend — the *prize*, the thing I held the gate for — was a *book.* …A *ledger.* *(his voice drops, reverent and savage)* …The *slaver's* *register.* The book of *names.* Every soul that *magistrate* held in *bondage* — *legally*, friend, with *seals* and *signatures*, hundreds of them — *existed*, as *property*, in *that* *one* *book.* It was the *deed* to *people.* The *paper* that made *owning* them *lawful.* *(barely)* …Burn the book, friend, and there's *no* *record*, no *proof*, no *legal* *claim* — and *hundreds* of *bound* souls become, *overnight*, in the eyes of the *very* *law* that *chained* them, *free.* *(he sits back, fierce and proud)* …*That's* what was in the uncrackable vault, friend. Not *treasure.* …*Bondage*, written down. …And we *stole* it, and we *burned* it, and *hundreds* of people *woke* up *owned* …and *went* to *sleep* *free* …and *never* *knew* *why.* *(quietly)* …*That's* what I held the gate for. …Not *loot.* …A *bonfire* of a *book* that *should* *never* have *existed.* …Tell me, friend — *(he smiles)* …*was* that worth an *old* *planner's* *last* *exit?* …I have *never*, *once*, *for* a *heartbeat* …thought it *wasn't.**

nodes.speaker: Greaves
nodes.text: *(His weathered face fills with a gratitude so deep it's nearly grief, and he reaches across and grips your cold hand in both his scarred ones.)* …You'd *carry* it. *(barely)* …Friend, I've been *trying* to give that boy this for an *age*, and I *can't* — I'm *right* *here*, *ten* *feet* from him, and I *can't*, because every time I *say* it he *hears* it as *me* being *kind*, *me* *letting* him *off*, and his *guilt* turns it *into* *proof* that it *was* his *fault* and I'm just too *good* to *say* so. *(fiercely)* …He needs to hear it from someone who *isn't* *me.* A *plain* soul, with *no* stake, who he *can't* argue is just being *gentle.* *(he gathers himself, and gives it to you, the plain true thing, the way he could never make Rook hear it)* …Tell Rook this, friend. *Exactly* this. *(slowly, each word weighed)* …*'Greaves did the math. You weren't a factor in it the way you think — he didn't stay to save you, special, out of all of them. He stayed because he was the oldest and the plan was run and the count was clear, and he'd have made the exact same call if it'd been a stranger's crew. It was never about you being worth more or him loving you most. It was arithmetic, clean and cold and right, done by the best planner who ever lived, and he was at peace with it before he ever touched the gate. You did not cost him his life, Rook. You did not fail to get him out. There was no out to get him to. He chose the only correct move in the job, the way he always did — and the one thing, the ONLY thing, that has ever made him regret it …is watching you carry a debt that was never yours. Set it down. Not for you. For HIM. He can't rest while you're breaking. Let the old man rest.'*"* *(he sits back, his eyes wet)* …*That's* the *word*, friend. …*Carry* it *true.* …And — *(barely)* …*walk* the *long* way *back* to me, after. …I want to *see* his *face* …when the *weight* comes *off.* …I've *waited* an *age* …to see that boy …*put* it *down.**

nodes.speaker: Greaves
nodes.text: *(He listens, and the fierce quiet pride in him rises to meet your words, and when you finish he nods, slow and deep and final, a craftsman hearing his life's best work correctly named at last.)* …*'The law you broke was the slaver's law — the same kind of law the Wall is.'* *(softly, fierce)* …Friend. …I *knew* it. In my *bones*, the night I took the gate, I *knew* there was no *crime* in it — but I'm a *planner*, not a *priest*, I never had the *words* for *why* a thing the *law* called *theft* felt, in my *chest*, like the most *lawful* thing I ever *did.* *(he grips the table)* …And you've *given* them. …A *ledger* that calls *owning* people *order* …and *freeing* them *theft* …is *not* a *law*, friend. …It's a *crime* with a *seal* on it. …And *breaking* it isn't *robbery.* …It's the *only* *justice* there *was.* *(his voice fills)* …And your *Wall*, friend — *(fierce now, leaning in)* …if it's what you say — a *ledger* that *holds* *souls* and calls the *holding* *order* — then it is the *same* *vault*, friend, the *exact* *same* *job*, only *bigger.* …And I'll tell you the thing a *dead* *planner* *knows* that *might* *save* your *life*: *(low, intent)* …*every* such vault has a *book* at the *heart* of it. …Not *gold* — *gold's* the *distraction.* …The *real* *prize*, the thing that makes the *bondage* *lawful*, is always a *ledger*, a *record*, a *paper* that says *'these are owed, these are bound, these are owned.'* …*Find* the *Wall's* *ledger*, friend. …The *thing* that *makes* its *cruelty* *legal.* …And *burn* it. *(fierce, proud, certain)* …*That's* the *job.* …Leave the *gold.* …*Burn* the *book* …that *should* *never* have *existed.* …And *hundreds* — *thousands* — of *bound* *souls* will *wake* up *owned* …and go to *sleep* *free.* *(he sits back, magnificent)* …*Go* and *do* the *last* *job*, friend. …The *real* one. …And know an *old* *planner* …would have *held* a *gate* …for *that.**

nodes.speaker: Greaves
nodes.text: *(He releases your hand and settles back, warm and steady, and nods you toward the wiry man at the bolthole's edge.)* …Go on, friend. *(a gentle rumble)* …And if you've taken my word for Rook — *(his weathered face goes soft)* …carry it *careful.* That boy's the *best* of us, whatever Vesper says with her *charm* and whatever *I* did with my *gate.* He's got the *truest* *heart* in this *crew*, which is *exactly* *why* he's *carrying* a *weight* that was never *his* — only a *true* heart *grieves* this *hard* for a thing it didn't *do.* *(quietly)* …Don't let him *argue* you out of it. He's *clever*, and *guilt* makes the *clever* *cleverer* at *staying* *guilty* — he'll *try* to *pick* *holes* in the *word*, to *prove* it's *still* his *fault.* *(he meets your eyes)* …*Don't* *let* him. …*Hold* the *word* *steady*, the way I *held* the *gate.* …*Plain.* *True.* *Immovable.* …*'There was no out to get him to.'* …Say it *however* many *times* it *takes.* …And when his *face* *finally* — *(his voice thickens)* …when the *weight* *comes* *off* him, friend …*that's* the *prize.* …*That's* the *real* *last* *job.* …*Freeing* the *one* *soul* …still *bound* …by *my* *gate.* …*Go.**

variants.text: *(The restless fingers are still. That is the first thing you notice — the lockpicks lie quiet in his open hand, no longer turning, turning, and Rook is sitting up straight, and the weight that bent his shoulders an age is simply gone, and when he looks at you his eyes are clear and wet and astonished.)* …He did the math. *(quietly, wondering)* …*'There was no out to get him to.'* …You said it, and you said it again when I tried to pick holes in it, and you *held* it steady, friend, the way *he* held the gate — and somewhere around the *fifth* time I stopped being able to *argue* with it. *(he laughs, shaky)* …Because it's *true.* I knew it was true the *whole* time, underneath. I just — I *needed* it to be my fault, friend, because *my fault* meant there was *something I could have done*, and *something I could have done* was *easier* to live with than *nothing* — than the old man just *choosing*, clean, and me *unable* to *stop* him. *(he presses the picks to his chest)* …But it *was* nothing. There *was* no lock to pick, that night. He didn't *die* of my *failure.* He *chose*, the way he chose everything, *right.* *(he looks toward Greaves, and his face breaks into something an age overdue)* …I can *look* at him now, friend. …For the *first* time since the gate …I can *look* at Greaves …and not *flinch.* …You picked it. …The one lock I couldn't. …*Thank* you.*

*(His sharp eyes catch on you the moment you approach — a thief's read, instant and total — and something in your bearing, some weight you're carrying, makes the restless fingers go still around the picks.)* …You've been talking to Greaves. *(flat, wary, the picks gripped tight)* …I can tell. You've got that — people get a *look*, after they talk to Greaves. Soft. Sorry-for-me. *(his jaw tightens)* …He gave you something to tell me. Didn't he. *(quietly, and there's a tremor under the flatness)* …He's been *trying* to give it to me an *age*, friend, whatever it is — some *kindness*, some *'it's not your fault, Rook'* — and I *can't* — *(he looks at the picks)* …I can't *take* it from *him*, friend, because it comes from *him*, and *he's* the one I — *(he stops)* …Say it, then. Whatever he gave you. …I won't promise to *believe* it. …But you're *plain*, friend, you're not *Vesper* … …maybe I can *hear* it from *plain.* …Go on. …What did the old man …tell you to tell me?*

*(The wiry man's fingers never stop moving over the picks, and his sharp eyes flick to you and away, restless as his hands.)* …Still here. *(a flat, quiet voice)* …Most cold ones drift off once they've had the *charming* one's stories. Vesper's the *show*; I'm just the *muscle* that opened the doors. *(he turns a pick over)* …But you came *back* this way, to the *quiet* corner, which means you're either *lost* or you're the *thorough* kind. *(a tired almost-smile)* …Thorough, I'd guess. You've the look of someone *casing* a job so big it scares you, and a *thorough* soul knows you don't case a job by talking to the *Face.* The Face tells you how it *should* go. *(quietly)* …You want to know how it *actually* goes, you talk to the *Hands.* …Because the *Hands* is the one *still* *in* the *room* …when the plan goes *wrong.* …Ask, then. …I'll tell you the part Vesper *charms* past. …I was *there* for the part …that *doesn't* make a good *story.**

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(He keeps working the picks, but he angles himself toward you, which from a man this closed is its own small opening.)* …Ask. *(quietly)* …But I'll warn you — I'm not the *talker*. I do better with *locks* than *words*; a lock you can *feel* your way through, but words you've got to — *(he frowns)* …words you've got to *mean*, and meaning them's the hard part. …Ask anyway. …I'll do my best to *feel* my way through.

choices.text: *(read the Hands — past the guilt, to the genius and the wound braided together)* "Let me look at you a moment, Rook. At the thing those restless hands can't pick."
"Tell me the part Vesper charms past, Rook. What actually happened at the gate."

*(try to lift the guilt yourself — that Greaves chose, that it was never Rook's to carry — without going glib)* "Rook — put the picks down a second and hear me. You've decided Greaves dying was a lock you failed to pick fast enough. It wasn't. There was no lock that night — there was a man doing arithmetic, choosing the only right move, the way he always did. You didn't fail him. You can't fail someone at a thing that was never yours to do. The guilt you're carrying isn't even *about* you, Rook — it's the last way you've found to keep arguing with a choice that was only ever his. Let it go. Not because you're owed peace. Because the guilt is a debt you assigned yourself for a crime that never happened."

*(deliver Greaves' word — the plain true thing, held steady)* "Rook. Greaves gave me a thing to tell you, and I'm going to say it plain, and I'm going to hold it steady however hard you argue, because that's what he asked. Here it is, exactly: *He did the math. He didn't stay to save you, special — he stayed because he was the oldest and the plan was run and the count was clear, and he'd have made the same call for a stranger's crew. You didn't cost him his life. You didn't fail to get him out. There was no out to get him to. And the only thing that ever made him regret it is watching you carry a debt that was never yours. Set it down — not for you, for him. He can't rest while you're breaking. Let the old man rest.*"

*(the one who knows the Wall would bill a grieving thief for a debt that isn't his)* "Rook — whatever else, hear this. The grey Wall I'm walking toward runs on debts — who owes, who's owed, every soul billed for what it failed to pay. And you've done its work *for* it: you've billed yourself for Greaves' death, assigned yourself a debt no one charged you, and you've been paying it an age. But a debt you invent to punish yourself is still the Wall's whole logic, Rook — the lie that every loss must be *someone's* fault, every grief a ledger to be balanced. Some things just *cost*, and no one owes for them. Greaves chose. The cost was his to pay and he paid it gladly. The only debt in this whole bolthole is the one you forged against yourself — and the bravest thing a soul who hates the Wall can do is refuse to run its arithmetic on his own heart."
"I'll come back, Rook. There's a lock here I mean to help you pick."

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(He lets you look, his fingers finally going still on the picks, and you let your cold move past the closed wariness to the soul that has been working one impossible lock for an age.)* *(And your sense finds genius and grief braided so tightly they have become the same strand. The genius first: your sense reads hands that are not merely skilled but *gifted* past explaining, a soul that understands locks the way a musician understands sound, that can *feel* the secret logic of any mechanism through fingertips alone — and reads that this gift was, in life, a joy, a pure delight in the solvable, in the click of a thing yielding up its inside. And your sense reads what the gift became, after the gate, and aches: that Rook has turned the whole of that gift inward, onto the one lock it cannot open — the past, the night, the gate, the question *could I have done something* — and has been picking at it, obsessively, an age, the way the gifted cannot leave a puzzle alone, except this puzzle has no solution and the picking only deepens the wound. Your sense reads the specific shape of his guilt, and finds it is the guilt of the *competent*: that Rook, whose whole identity is that there is no lock he can't open, no problem he can't solve, cannot bear a night where the problem — *get Greaves out* — had *no solution*, because to accept that is to accept that his gift had a *limit*, that there was a door even the best Hands who ever lived could not open. And so he has, perversely, *chosen* the guilt, because guilt says *I could have done something, I just failed* — and *failure* he can live with, *failure* is a lock he might someday pick — but *helplessness*, the truth that there was *nothing to be done*, is the one thing his competent soul cannot accept. Your sense reads, last, the cruelty of it: that Greaves chose freely and is at peace, and the only person still imprisoned by the gate is Rook, who keeps it locked himself, from the inside, because the alternative to guilt is the unbearable truth that the man he loved simply *chose* to die for him and there was not one single thing his clever hands could have done about it. He is not waiting to be forgiven. Your sense reads it clear: he is waiting for permission to stop needing to have been able to save him.)*

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(His hands go still, and his voice when it comes is flat and precise, a man reciting a thing he has gone over so many times the words have worn smooth.)* …The gate. *(quietly)* …Vesper tells it like a *triumph* — *'and we got out clean with the prize.'* And we *did.* That part's true. What she *charms* past is the *count.* *(he sets the picks down)* …Three doors, the clockwork lock. I had it — I had it *open*, friend, all three, faster than I'd ever — I was *good* that night, the *best* I ever was. And the *failsafe* dropped. The gate. The maker's nasty little secret. And it had to be *held*, by *hand*, against the *mechanism*, or it'd *seal* us *all* in the *hall* with the *alarm* *screaming.* *(barely)* …And I — friend, I *do* *locks.* That's what I *do.* I went *straight* at the *gate-mechanism* — I thought, *I'll pick it, I'll jam it, I'll find the catch and we all walk* — and Greaves — *(his voice catches)* …Greaves saw me go for it, and he saw something I didn't, he saw it *faster*, because he was the *planner* and he saw the *whole shape* — he saw there *wasn't* a catch, that the mechanism was *one-way*, that *no* hand however *clever* was *picking* that gate, that the *only* way was for someone to *bodily hold it* while the rest got through. *(he looks at his hands)* …And he *grabbed* me, friend. Threw me at the *opening.* And I — I had my *picks* out, I was *reaching* *back* for the *mechanism*, *certain* I could — and he *smiled* at me, and he said *'not this one, Rook — this one's not a lock'* — *(barely)* …and he *took* the *gate.* …And the *last* thing I saw of *Greaves* …was him *holding* a *door* *shut* …that I was *still* *sure*, friend, even as the *prize* burned and we *ran* …I was *still* *sure* …I could have *picked.* …That's the part Vesper charms past. …The part where the best Hands who ever lived …reached for a lock …that wasn't *there.**

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(You don't go glib, and you don't say 'it's not your fault' — you name the exact mechanism of it, that he chose guilt because guilt meant a solvable failure and helplessness was the one lock he couldn't accept, and you hold it steady, and a man who feels his way through locks feels the truth of it click home.)* *(His clever hands go utterly still. And then they begin, very slightly, to shake.)* …*'You can't fail someone at a thing that was never yours to do.'* *(barely)* …friend. …how did you — *(he presses the heels of his hands to his eyes)* …that's it. that's the — I never *said* it, not even to *myself*, but that's *exactly* it: I'd *rather* it be my *fault* than my *helplessness.* because my *fault* means I *could* have — and *could-have* is a *lock*, friend, a *lock* I might *someday* — and *helplessness* — *(his voice breaks)* …*helplessness* means there was a *door* the *best* *hands* *alive* *couldn't* *open*, and I have spent my *whole* *life* being the man there's *no* *door* he *can't* — *(he lowers his hands, wrecked)* …and you're saying the *gate* wasn't a *door* I *failed.* …it was a *door* that *wasn't* *mine.* …it was *Greaves'* *choice*, and *choices* aren't *locks*, you can't *pick* them, they're not *yours* to *pick* — *(a shaking breath)* …all this time I thought the guilt was me *honoring* him. and it was just me — *refusing* to *accept* that my *hands* had a *limit*, and *spending* his *death* to *avoid* it. *(he looks at you, and an age of weight shifts)* …god, friend. …I made his *gift* — the *biggest* thing he ever *did* — into a *referendum* on *my* *fingers.* …he'd *hate* that. …he'd *laugh* at me and *hate* that. *(a wet, broken laugh)* …okay. …*okay.* …say it once more, friend. the *can't-fail-someone* part. …I think I can — …I think the *tumblers* are *finally* — …say it once more, and I think the lock *turns.**

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(You name it carefully — that the guilt is a debt he forged himself, that there was no lock that night — and he listens, hands still, and something in him eases, though years of holding it don't let go all at once.)* …*'A debt you invented.'* *(slowly)* …That lands, friend. I'll not pretend it doesn't. …You didn't do the *thing* — the *'it's not your fault, Rook'* thing that Vesper does, that just *slides* off me. You said something with *gears* in it. *Mechanism.* *Why* I chose it. *(he turns a pick over, thinking)* …I need to sit with the gears a while. An age of guilt doesn't pick loose in one go — you of all souls should know a *good* lock takes *time* and the *right* *feel*, not *force.* *(a tired, real almost-smile)* …But you found the *first* *tumbler*, friend. I *felt* it give. …Come back. …Say it again, the *gears* version, not the *comfort* version. …I think if you *work* it a few more times, *steady* … …I think this lock might *open.* …And it's the only one, friend, in my *whole* *life* … …that ever *beat* me. …Come back and help me *beat* it.*

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(You reach to lift it — but it comes out as reassurance, *it's not your fault, you did everything you could*, and a man who has heard exactly those words from Vesper an age too many feels them slide right off.)* …Don't. *(quietly, not unkind)* …*'It's not your fault. You did all you could.'* …Friend, I've *had* that one. Vesper's *worn* it *smooth* on me. *(he shakes his head)* …It doesn't *work*, and I'll tell you *why*, since you tried honest: because *'you did all you could'* still says there was a *could* — still treats that night like a *job* I *might* have *done* *better* — and so my *brain*, friend, my *thief's* *brain* that *can't* leave a *lock* alone, just goes *right* back to *picking* at *what* the *could* *was.* *(he turns the picks over)* …You can't *comfort* a man *out* of a *lock*, friend. …You have to show him the *lock* *isn't* *there.* …Come back when you can do *that* — when you can make me *see* there was *nothing* to *pick*, not that I *picked* it *badly.* …*Nothing.* …*That's* the only thing that'd — *(he stops)* …that's the only key that *fits*, friend. …And *comfort* …*comfort's* the *wrong* *shape.**

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong — you reach for the easy thing, that Greaves *wanted* to do it, that it was *noble*, a hero's sacrifice — and you watch the word land like a dropped pick on stone.)* …*Noble.* *(flat, cold)* …A *sacrifice.* *(he sets the picks down very precisely)* …Friend. Do you know what that does? *(quietly, hard)* …When you call it *noble*, a *sacrifice*, a *hero's* *death* — you make it a thing he *gave.* A *gift.* And a *gift*, friend — a *gift* has a *receiver.* And the *receiver* of Greaves' *noble* *sacrifice* …*was* *me.* *(his jaw is tight)* …So now it's not just that he *died.* Now he died *for* me, *gave* me his *life* like a *purse*, and I'm supposed to — what, friend? *Spend* it *well?* Be *worth* it? *(barely)* …You've just handed me a *debt* with his *face* on it. *'Greaves gave his life* for *you, Rook, what have you* done *to* deserve *it'* — and that's *worse*, friend, that's the *whole* *weight* *doubled*, because now I'm not just *guilty* I couldn't *save* him, I *owe* him a *life* I can *never* *pay* *back.* *(he picks the lockpicks back up, closing off)* …Go. …And don't come back with *noble.* …*Noble* makes it a *debt.* …If you want to help me, friend — *(barely)* …find a way to tell me it was *nobody's* *to* *give* and *nobody's* *to* *owe.* …That it just — *happened*, clean, his *choice*, *no* *bill* *attached.* …*That* I could maybe — …but *noble?* …*noble* just *locks* it *tighter.**

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(You say it plain, all of it, and when he tries — once, twice — to pick a hole in it, to find the angle that keeps it his fault, you simply say it again, steady, immovable, the way Greaves held the gate. And somewhere around the third time, the wiry man stops arguing, because there is nothing left to argue, and a lock an age old finally, finally turns.)* …*'There was no out to get him to.'* *(barely)* …he — he *said* that? *those* *words?* *(his hands are shaking)* …because that's — friend, that's the *one* thing, the *exact* thing — *'no out to get him to'* means there was *no* *door*, no *lock*, *nothing* my *hands* could have — it wasn't that I was *slow* or *wrong* or *failed*, it was that the *job* had *no* *solution*, and a *job* with *no* *solution* isn't a *failure*, it's just — *(a wet, wrecked, dawning laugh)* …it's just *true.* *(he presses the picks to his chest)* …and *'he'd have made the same call for a stranger's crew'* — that — *(his voice breaks open)* …that's the part that — because I *made* it *about* *me*, friend, I made his *whole* *death* a thing about *whether* *I* was *worth* it, and he's — he's saying it was never *about* me at *all*, it was *arithmetic*, *clean*, the kind he did a *hundred* *times*, and I just — *happened* to be *one* of the *numbers.* *(he looks up, and an age of weight is sliding off him)* …*'he can't rest while you're breaking.'* *(barely)* …oh. …*oh.* …I thought the guilt was me *keeping faith* with him. …and it was *keeping him tied* to that gate. …my *grief* was the *one* *thing* …still *holding* the *door* *shut.* *(he stands, unsteady, and looks across the bolthole at Greaves, and his face does a thing it has not done in an age)* …friend. …I have to — I have to go *look* at him. …*really* look. …for the *first* time since the gate. …and you — *(he grips your arm, fierce, wet)* …you tell your *Wall*, when you get there, that the best *Hands* who ever *lived* says: …*some doors aren't locks.* …*some losses aren't debts.* …*some things just cost, and no one owes.* …and the *bravest* *pick* a thief ever *makes* …is *refusing* to *bill* his *own* *heart* …for a *crime* that *never* *happened.* …now *go.* …I've got a *gate* to *finally* *walk* *back* *through.**

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(He listens, hands going still, and the flat wariness cracks, and what's under it is rawer than any of Vesper's charm or Greaves' calm — the open wound of the one who couldn't save someone.)* …*'A debt you forged against yourself.'* *(barely)* …friend. …you've — nobody's said it with the *gears* showing before. they all do the *comfort.* you did the — you showed me the *mechanism.* *(his hands tremble on the picks)* …that every loss must be *someone's* *fault* … that's the *Wall's* *lie*, you said. and I've been — I've been running the *Wall's* *arithmetic* on my *own* *heart*, an *age* of it, *billing* myself for a *death* nobody *charged* me for. *(a shaking breath)* …and the bravest thing a soul who *hates* the Wall can do …is *refuse* to *run* its *math* on *himself.* *(he looks at his clever, restless, guilty hands)* …I do *locks*, friend. I should have *seen* it. *guilt's* just a *lock* I built on the *inside*, to keep myself *in* — because *in*, with the *guilt*, at least there was a *job*, a *could-have*, a thing to *pick* at. …*out* …*out* is just *Greaves* *gone* and *no* *reason* and *nothing* I could've *done.* *(barely)* …I think — friend, I think I need a thing I don't have yet, to open it all the way. some *word*, some — *(he looks at you)* …but you've shown me the *lock* is *mine.* on the *inside.* and a lock on the inside, friend — *(a wet, fierce, almost-smile)* …a lock on the inside, the *Hands* can *always* *reach.* …come *back.* …I think you and I …are going to *pick* this one *together.* …it's the *only* one …that ever *beat* me. …and you just showed me …it's been *unlocked* …from the *outside* …the *whole* *time.**

nodes.speaker: Rook
nodes.text: *(He nods, and his restless fingers resume their work on the picks, but he watches you go with something less closed than before.)* …Go on, friend. *(quietly)* …And — if you've not been to *Greaves* yet — *(his jaw works)* …go to him. He'll — *(a flicker)* …he'll tell you he's *fine*, and the *worst* thing, friend, the thing I *can't* — is that he *means* it. He *is* fine. He made his *peace* with that gate before he ever *touched* it. *(barely)* …It's just *me* that's not fine. The *one* of us who *walked* *out* …is the *only* one still *trapped* in there. *(he turns a pick over)* …If he gives you something — *(he stops)* …if the old man gives you some *word* to carry back to me, friend … *(his voice drops)* …I'll *tell* you straight, I'll probably *try* to *pick* it *apart*, find the angle that keeps it my fault — that's just what I *do*, I can't *leave* a lock alone, even *this* one. *(he meets your eyes, and there's a plea under the wariness)* …so if you carry it … *carry* it *steady*, friend. …Don't let me *argue* you *out* of it. …*Hold* it …the way *he* held the *gate.* …I think that might be …the only way …it ever *gets* through.*
